annotations.label: Lebesgue integral

anchor.snippet: The Lebesgue integral , named after French mathematician Henri Lebesgue , is one way to make this concept rigorous

annotations.note: Mathlib's `MeasureTheory.integral` is the Bochner generalization of the Lebesgue integral and `MeasureTheory.lintegral` handles the non-negative case.

annotations.label: Lebesgue integration (term)

anchor.snippet: The term Lebesgue integration can mean either the general theory of integration of a function with respect to a general measure

annotations.note: Integration against a general measure is captured by `MeasureTheory.lintegral` (non-negative) and `MeasureTheory.integral` (Bochner).

anchor.snippet: Define the distribution function of f as the "width of a slab"

annotations.note: Mathlib has the layer-cake formula `lintegral_eq_lintegral_meas_le` using the level sets μ{f > t}, but no top-level definition named `distributionFunction`.

anchors.snippet: Define the distribution function of f as the "width of a slab"

annotations.label: Measure space

anchor.snippet: We start with a measure space ( E , X , μ ) where E is a set

annotations.note: `MeasureSpace` is defined as a `MeasurableSpace` together with a `volume` measure.

annotations.label: Measurable function

anchor.snippet: A real-valued function f on E is measurable if the pre-image of every interval of the form ( t , ∞) is in X

annotations.note: `Measurable f` says preimages of all measurable sets are measurable; for ℝ-valued functions this matches the (t, ∞)-preimage definition.

anchors.snippet: A real-valued function f on E is measurable if the pre-image of every interval of the form ( t , ∞) is in X

annotations.label: Equivalence with Borel preimages and closure under limits

anchor.snippet: We can show that this is equivalent to requiring that the pre-image of any Borel subset of R be in X

annotations.note: Closure under suprema/limits is `Measurable.iSup`/`Measurable.iInf`, and ℝ's Borel σ-algebra is the default `BorelSpace ℝ` instance making (t,∞)-preimage equivalence built in.

anchors.snippet: We can show that this is equivalent to requiring that the pre-image of any Borel subset of R be in X

annotations.label: Integral of an indicator function

anchor.snippet: To assign a value to the integral of the indicator function 1 S of a measurable set S consistent with the given measure μ

annotations.note: `lintegral_indicator` reduces ∫⁻ 1_S to a restricted measure, giving μ(S) via `setLIntegral_one`.

annotations.label: Lebesgue integrable function (L^1)

anchor.snippet: we say that f is Lebesgue integrable

annotations.note: `MeasureTheory.Integrable f μ` means f is AE strongly measurable with finite integral of `‖f‖`, the L¹ definition.

annotations.label: Lebesgue integral via improper Riemann integral

anchor.snippet: The Lebesgue integral may then be defined as the improper Riemann integral of f ∗

annotations.note: Mathlib does not define the Lebesgue integral as an improper Riemann integral, but `IntegralEqImproper` proves the resulting equality for integrable functions.

anchors.snippet: The Lebesgue integral may then be defined as the improper Riemann integral of f ∗

annotations.label: Integral of a complex-valued function

anchor.snippet: If h = f + ig for real-valued integrable functions f , g , then the integral of h is defined by

annotations.note: `integral_re_add_im` proves ∫ f = (∫ re f) + i · (∫ im f); the Bochner `integral` already handles ℂ-valued functions directly.

annotations.label: Complex Lebesgue integrability via absolute value

anchor.snippet: The function is Lebesgue integrable if and only if its absolute value is Lebesgue integrable

annotations.note: `integrable_norm_iff` shows Integrable f ↔ Integrable ‖f‖, which for ℂ gives the |·| version.

annotations.label: Dirichlet function: not Riemann-integrable but Lebesgue-integrable

anchor.snippet: Consider the indicator function of the rational numbers, 1 Q , also known as the Dirichlet function

annotations.note: No file/decl in Mathlib explicitly states the Dirichlet function as a non-Riemann/yes-Lebesgue example.

annotations.label: Failure of monotone convergence for Riemann integral

anchor.snippet: As shown above, the indicator function 1 Q on the rationals is not Riemann integrable

annotations.note: No explicit Mathlib example demonstrating failure of monotone convergence for the Riemann integral was located.

anchors.snippet: As shown above, the indicator function 1 Q on the rationals is not Riemann integrable

anchor.snippet: Two functions are said to be equal almost everywhere

annotations.note: Mathlib writes `f =ᵐ[μ] g` for `EventuallyEq (ae μ) f g`, the almost-everywhere equality.

annotations.label: Integral respects almost-everywhere equality (non-negative)

anchor.section: Basic theorems of the Lebesgue integral
anchor.snippet: If f and g are non-negative measurable functions (possibly assuming the value +∞ ) such that f = g almost everywhere

annotations.note: `lintegral_congr_ae` gives `f =ᵐ[μ] g → ∫⁻ f ∂μ = ∫⁻ g ∂μ`.

annotations.label: Integrability respects almost-everywhere equality

anchor.section: Basic theorems of the Lebesgue integral
anchor.snippet: If f and g are functions such that f = g almost everywhere, then f is Lebesgue integrable if and only if g is Lebesgue integrable

annotations.note: `integrable_congr` proves `f =ᵐ[μ] g → (Integrable f μ ↔ Integrable g μ)`.

annotations.label: Linearity of the Lebesgue integral

anchor.snippet: Linearity : If f and g are Lebesgue integrable functions and a and b are real numbers

annotations.note: `integral_add` and `integral_smul` together give linearity on integrable functions.
